speaker: interviewer
text: Lilit Pohlmann in London, England. This is take nine. You were just saying-- you were telling us about your husband-- your late husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about Eric. I was saying, yes, this was the greatest sadness. I think after my father, my brother, that was the greatest sadness of my life. Because you see, we were not very long together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it seemed like it's 30-- it's been 30 years, yeah? You see, with this man, to have known him was to have loved him. Anybody-- man, woman, child-- make not the slightest difference. Because he had something in him that was so warm and so-- and the wisdom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so every minute was like an hour and every hour was like a day. And so, you know, he certainly appreciated every moment of life. And he made you aware of it. And he never took the slightest thing for granted. It was just-- consequently, no-- no matter how short a time we were together, it seems like we've been together for-- for years and years and years. And there isn't a day that I do not think about him. And-- and you know, don't feel about him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible sadness. I mean, the festival stopped, of course. And what can I say? It was-- there was a service there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not get a rabbi because the rabbi wouldn't come for the cremation, so the colleagues-- that-- just everything. And then, I-- well, had to bring his ashes back. Karen came immediately. She came. I remember she had-- her arm was in plaster. She had broken her arm but never told me she was in London working. And we were in [INAUDIBLE]. She didn't want to worry me. But she had a feeling-- something at 10 o'clock in the morning told her to call me. And she never, ever, ever called from work-- never called me during the day. She'd call either first thing, or we would call her first thing in the morning. Or she'd call last thing in the evening. Or we-- I don't know-- as it was arranged. But 10 o'clock in the morning, she called. That was the moment he died. It was terrible for her. She was so broken-hearted. This is why, afterwards, she went to America. She just could not be here, around. And I was in a pretty miserable state. Probably not very easy to live with, I don't know. Not a very happy person. Which, normally, I am not an unhappy person.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was that. And-- and what else can I tell you? From then on, life continues and Karen, meantime, was married once. Unfortunately, it lasted eight-- eight years and was divorced. Was very unhappy-- totally, totally distraught about that. But luckily, there were no children from that marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she remarried again and is very happy with-- both of them are happy with one another. Thank God. Touch wood. [LAUGHS] And now they have four children. One boy is an adopted boy. It took a long time to get the adoption through, but now it's all legalized and so forth-- an adopted boy. And three are her own. So she has three boys, and the last one is a little girl. And the little girl is now seven months. That's all. My mother-- they live in Florida. In Florida, in America, which is, of course, much, much too far from-- from us. I used to go out and see her. My mother-- we used to go together. And so with Peter-- my partner-- present partner. But ever since about a year and-- well, nearly two years now, it is an impossibility because my mother is now 89 years old. And she's ailing. And-- and I cannot leave her and I cannot have strange people just look after her and I should distance myself at such a distance, like from here to Florida, which is very sad. My mother, unfortunately, had a-- lost her second husband, also not too long. They were married for a number of years, but he died much too young, like my husband-- my late husband. Much too young. And so she's been, now, for 33 years, alone. 33 years. And so I couldn't possibly distance myself from her. She had an accident last year in September and has never been quite the same since. And that's a great pity because she was a remarkable lady-- very brave. Very brave, very energetic, totally independent. Very clever, very wise. And very beautiful. Very beautiful person, very elegant person. And it is heartbreaking what happens to people in age. It's heartbreaking. [DRINKS] [CRIES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Their independence goes. The primary thing-- that they have to be dependent on others. It's the saddest thing. What else would you like to know about Peter? Something else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, Peter and I have now been together for, I think, longer than I've been with my ex-husband. And certainly longer than I've been with Eric. This is our 11th year together, although we're not married. But not because he wouldn't have wished it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it so happens-- I mean, how many times can you get married? So it's perfectly fine. Not expecting any more children, so it's all right. We're happy. He's a very nice man-- particularly nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't suppose I could be with anybody else really. He is a very caring person. He's a very upright person. Very honest, cultured, intelligent, and then-- well, warm, loving. And not only to me, but very, very kind and considerate and very understanding to my mother, which means a lot to me-- a lot. And of course, to my daughter, also. But particularly to my mother. I mean, he has quite a lot to put up with nowadays. It's not easy for him. And he doesn't have very much joy out of me because I'm hardly here. I-- I'm mostly at my mother's and with her and doing for her. And when I do get home, I'm not very conducive to-- to-- to high brow conversations or-- I'm usually very tired, very exhausted. And-- and it shows. But he understands and he, I hope, can cope with it. I mean, he would love to travel. He can now. He is-- by profession, he is in publishing. He's, all his life, in publishing. And he is a literary agent. He was a literary agent for a number of very prominent authors, among them Ian Fleming, among them Eric Ambler, Kingsley Amis. I could name quite a few. But neither here nor there. But at the moment, I mean, he's only looking after one because he has too much on otherwise. He's looking after Richard Holmes, who-- I don't know. You may or may not know. He's the biographer-- he's the biographer of, they say, the century now. Peter actually really made him because he made him sit and write. And he said not to worry about anything else. Not to worry about food, money, just write. And he did, indeed. With the very first book he wrote, he had the Somerset Maugham Prize. And so it goes on. I mean, he is a very clever writer. And Peter sort of-- and then, apart from that, he is a trustee for He's a trustee-- he's not a trustee-- he is a director of Agatha Christie, LTD. He has a lot of-- he's a trustee and treasurer of the Royal Literary Fund.

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a lot of-- he's very busy and has a lot of activities. And it's, you know, all in publishing. And his name is a well-known name in publishing. What can I say? That's professionally speaking. But mostly and predominantly, he's a very, very kind and good man. And I suppose I'm very lucky. Very lucky. Very privileged. What else would you like to know? When you think back over your-- over everything that you went through-- what do you think are the reasons for you survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I could never possibly attempt to answer a question like this. It's beyond me. I asked myself not once-- and like many people do-- why-- why-- why I? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it a coincidence? Is it all a matter of-- it was written that I should and my little brother should not? And-- and thousands and thousands and millions of others should not? I mean, I have no answer to that. I really don't know. I'm not--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: as religious as-- as that. And possibly, spiritually speaking, maybe there is a message that I should give. Maybe I should give something of myself now to-- to-- to the posterity or-- I have no idea. I try. I do my best. I-- I know that it's-- to have survived all of this, well, what is the word? Is that a privilege? Is that a privilege? I don't know. Maybe it's a terrible handicap because one carries, forever, a certain aspect of almost guilt in oneself that it's not right I should be here and not the others. But here I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Here but for the-- but for the-- you know. The grace of God.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grace of-- yes. Grace of God. But why? I-- I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my-- myself-- sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I was just going to ask--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are-- we are the only two survivors of the family-- extended family, albeit. But family of approximately 380 people on my mother's side. On my father's side, I must admit, I didn't know many. I knew my grandmother very briefly when I was very, very young child. But I just remember her briefly because I go quite a long time back in my memory. I remember-- I knew his brother, whom I met only in Israel when I went to Israel because he went to Palestine as a young boy like my father wanted to and never managed, for many reasons. But primarily because he had an accident-- fractured his knee-- and was invalid for quite some time. And he considered that such people are not needed to build a country. So this is why he didn't go in the first place. But then-- anyway, I knew his brother and I knew his sister and that's all. I don't know if he had any other family or not. My mother, on the other hand-- as I said, she had quite an extended.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of aunts and uncles and they had children and children and children and cousins and God knows. We are the only two alive. I mean, who survived the war. So are you asking me why? I don't know why. I was wondering, I suppose, if you think there's anything about your personality or maybe the fact that you were quite often with your mother-- whether you think any of those factors played a part.

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly. Possibly. Or-- but I think, far more, I cannot take any credit-- any credit whatsoever for my personality helping me, in the sense that-- of my clever ideas. No-- no. It was all so accidental. Pure accidents. And at some moments, we had occasions of which-- I could not speak about every single thing, but there were occasions where I really thought these people suddenly went blind and deaf. Because a minute ago they heard a noise here and heard this. They-- they must have. They couldn't have not.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yet, they turned around and went away. So was it God's hand? Maybe. And if so, why? I don't know why. I can't answer that. I think it would be presumptuous if I did. Before we finish and then go on to the photos and bring Peter in, is there anything else that you would like to add? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like what? In what-- in what-- in what direction?

speaker: interviewer
text: In any. In any sense. Anything that's been missing-- that this wouldn't feel complete if you don't say it now. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's difficult for me to-- no, but I mean, I tried to tell you, in detail-- and possibly not in so much detail because it could take forever. Everybody has so much to tell. But no, I-- I think I'm-- I'm pretty-- whatever I said to you, I think, is adequate for the purpose. What I possibly will say is that I'm very, very privileged to be able to do this for the Foundation. And I want to thank these people for having established such a foundation so that we-- the few of us who are still here and who still have the memory in us to convey and to-- to-- who remember, possibly precisely, or if not totally precisely, but who do remember facts-- things that happened. That they shouldn't be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because if it weren't for such a foundation, or people like that, who? History might really never know. And there are-- thank you to Steven Spielberg's foundation, thank you to the Holocaust Foundation. Thank you to all those people who sometimes, really, dedicate their lives to it, let alone-- let alone their-- I don't know their livelihood, but-- but their lives. And that is wonderful, I think, on their part.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very important. It's very important that history should know what happened, even though, unfortunately, history does repeat itself. It would be-- it would be-- I wish I could end on the note of saying that sometimes the wisdoms that we know-- like history repeats itself-- that it wouldn't exist. I wish that history would not repeat itself because we see what happens nowadays. And we would have imagined, in 1946, nobody would have-- would have, in their wildest imagination, conceived that such things as are happening today-- ethnic cleansing and what-- can ever happen again after such a Holocaust. And yet, they do. All the neo-Nazism, all the-- all the ethnic cleansings, all the killings in the Rwanda and wherever. So history, unfortunately, does repeat itself. And my great wish would be that it shouldn't, for posterity's sake, for the young people of this world. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Pohlmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for giving me your time. Thank you. Very, very interesting. Thank you. This is my grandmother, of whom I talked before. My mother's mother, that is-- the poor lady who committed suicide in the burning ghetto. When this photograph was taken, I can't tell. She might have been in her 20s here. Just for information, her name was Hinde Brueck. This is my grandfather, Aba Brueck, and myself, aged approximately two, I should think, in front of the house where my grandparents lived in Lwow. And by the way, where I was born. Because I was born in my grandparents' bed. That's it. Maybe we should first-- ah, this is my paternal grandmother with me again. And I think I must have been about a year or just under a year. I don't know. And her name was Regina Stern. This photograph was taken in Poronin, just outside Zakopane, which is in the mountains-- Polish Tatra mountains on our summer holiday 1938. My little brother is on the right with the tie. And his name was Uriel. And he must-- he would have been just under three years old. On the left, you see myself. And in the middle, this was the sort of fashion. They had teddy bears walking around children. And he was very scared. Not I, but he was very scared, which shows in his face. The top photograph shows my parents. My mother, Cecylia Abraham-- then Brueck, and shortly to be Stern. This was-- and my father, Filip. This was their engagement photograph. She was 18, 19. He was 21. The bottom photograph-- the bottom photograph here shows our family unit-- the one and only photograph that we have of its kind. This is my-- these are my parents-- my mother, my father, my little brother, and myself. This photograph was taken very shortly before the outbreak of the 1939 war in Krakow in gardens of Krakow in the middle of town known as [NON-ENGLISH]. This is rather a significant photograph because it is the last one taken about-- not quite a month before my little brother was taken away. It was taken in July of 1942 in Lwow, where we lived-- the street-- the house where we lived. And it is with me. This is a photograph of equal significance. It is taken at the Ukrainian convent, an orphanage where my mother and I were in hiding toward the end of our war, in 1943, '44. I'm here with another Jewish girl in [INAUDIBLE]. Her name was Zosia Anacht. She now lives in Israel. We are in a costume-- in the-- in a national Ukrainian outfit-- costume. And this is almost like going to a communion. Not exactly. On the right-- the photograph on the right shows the abbess-- the Mother Superior of the orphanage and convent where my mother and I were during the war-- toward the end of the war. And the sister next to her is Sister Anastasia, who was part of the-- of the staff, so to speak. One of the many sisters. But she was special, in as much that she helped us Jewish girls enormously. She was very kind, very good, very understanding. And full of fun, always. This photograph shows the person who saved us and to whom we owe our lives. This is Frau Weith-- Frau Irmegaord Wieth. The small photograph on the left shows her the way we knew her during the war when we were in hiding with her. This is what she looked like-- an epitome of a young Katharine Hepburn. The large photograph shows her when she's being presented in New York at the Israeli Consulate by the Israeli Console with the highest accolade and award-- the Medal for the Righteous in the World. This was in Krakow after the war-- 1945-- my mother and I. This is a photograph taken in London of my mother and myself. It could be late 1950s, 1960. This is a photocopy of a birth certificate which my mother received from Mother Superior at the convent-- Ukrainian convent during our stay there. And it must have been 1943, '44. This is an authentic-- this was an authentic birth certificate. And she carried this authentic name, and so by Julia Popovycz. That was her name in the convent. This is a Ukrainian identification-- identity card, if you like. Ukrainian. During-- taken during the time of my mother's stay in the convent under the name of Julia Popovycz. This is the actual paper-- I mean, this is actually-- sorry-- the photocopy of the actual Ukrainian identification paper for my mother during the war, with her photograph, the way she looked and the name of Julia Popovycz When she was in her convent. This is a police registration-- a Ukrainian police registration for my mother in Lwow under the name of Julia Popovycz when she lived in [? Kruchinski ?] Street. When she was born, where she worked, et cetera, et cetera. It's a photocopy of the actual document. This is a certificate stating-- sorry, a certificate from the chief rabbinate of-- from Warsaw, dated 1945-- July, 1945. Signed by Rabbi Horowitz-- Soloman Horowitz. Stating that my father, Filip Stern, born 22 June, 1904 in [NON-ENGLISH] died-- perished-- murdered by the Germans on the 15th of August, 1942. This is a letter written by Dr.-- by-- sorry-- by Rabbi Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] Schonfeld, as signed, to the Metropolitan Police in Piccadilly Place, London West 1, saying that my mother, Mrs. Cecylia Abraham, is-- came to this country on a domestic permit. Stating the number-- registration number, et cetera, et cetera. The permit was granted to the [? Hasmonean ?] Grammar School for whom Rabbi Dr. Schonfeld was a principal. And that some of-- of their pupils are living at 521 Finchley Road, NW6, and we desire-- they desire to transfer my mother from the school to that address. This is a so-called alien certificate of registration. When my mother arrived in this country, this was the document she received. She was here, as stated, on a domestic permit. These photographs were taken with my late husband, Eric-- Eric Pohlmann. The large one in the middle was taken at [INAUDIBLE] in Surrey, I believe-- in England-- in in September, 1976. The top one was taken in Israel in 1977. The bottom one was taken at some first night-- I can't remember-- theatrical first night-- also in 1977. This photograph is of my mother and myself in her favorite holiday resort in Switzerland, called Flims. This was the last time we could go there, which was two years ago-- two and a half years ago. She couldn't go any-- anymore because she's not well enough. This photograph depicts four generations. There's my mother, myself, my daughter, and my daughter's four children, one of whom-- the dark boy-- the adopted one. This was taken in the summer of 1944-- 19-- sorry-- 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their names-- the children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The children's names-- starting from the oldest is the adopted boy, Christopher Robin, who is now nine and a half years old. Karen's first boy-- her own is Corey-- Corey Alexander. He's four and a half years old. Her second boy is Daniel-- Daniel Oliver. He is now three and a half years old nearly-- three and a bit. And the latest prodigy is the baby-- baby Kaelin Tani and she is seven months old now.
